topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery opening was a parade of self-important patrons and pretentious chatter. I stood near my latest canvas, a swirling depiction of urban decay rendered in harsh, visceral tones. Then I saw it. A faint, almost hidden line of ink tracing the edge of my composition—a delicate, stylized depiction of a key. My breath hitched, a sudden, cold clench in my chest. I turned slowly, my gaze locking onto the source. It was Marcus, the perpetually smug critic whose reviews always seemed to imply my work was derivative. He was leaning against a pillar, a thin, self-satisfied smile playing on his lips, his arm casually draped near the frame. When his eyes met mine, there was a flicker—a knowing, almost predatory glint—before he smoothly shifted his weight, his attention already elsewhere. I felt a tightening in my jaw, a deep, unpleasant churning in my stomach. I didn't move, just watched him, cataloging the audacity of the gesture.

[story 2]

I spent the afternoon meticulously cleaning brushes, trying to scrub the lingering feeling of being undermined from my hands. It was the audacity of it that truly grated on me; the implication that someone else claimed ownership over my expression. Later, while packing up, I noticed it on the back of the largest piece—a small, intricate mandala, executed in a style I vaguely recognized as Marcus’s signature aesthetic. My fingers froze above the canvas. A sharp, hot sensation rose up my neck. I walked over, my footsteps deliberately heavy on the polished floor. When I reached the piece, I didn't touch it, but I leaned in close enough to see the detail. Marcus was across the room, laughing loudly with a collector, his posture radiating effortless superiority. I straightened up, pulling my jacket tighter around myself. The air suddenly felt too thin, and I could taste a bitter metallic tang on my tongue.

[story 3]

The sheer nerve of it made my skin crawl. I had spent weeks pouring my very essence onto that portrait, a raw depiction of isolation. To find a deliberate mark, an unmistakable signature of another hand woven into the fabric of my creation, felt like a profound insult. I paced the studio floor, the silence amplifying the frantic drumming in my ears. Every shadow seemed to deepen, pressing in on me. When I finally confronted the reality, I found the tattoo—a stylized raven, undeniably Marcus’s personal motif—etched subtly into the lower corner of the painting. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, hot and immediate. I stopped pacing, planting my feet firmly. I looked toward the door, not to flee, but to assess the distance between myself and the source of this unwelcome claim. The tension radiating off me was palpable, a stiff, unyielding barrier.
